CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- ship: Review all uncommitted changes, write a commit message, commit, and push — no co-author line.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.

- caveman:compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman:compress <filepath> or "compress memory file"

Now I have a complete picture of the codebase. Here is the full technical assessment.

---

# Technical Assessment: auth-api Rails Service

## Overview

A Rails 8.0 API-only authentication microservice for what appears to be a medical/healthcare platform ("ThatCookiesMine" is mentioned in password reset email copy, but comments reference "medical app requirements" and "clinical settings"). It handles registration, login, logout, token refresh, token validation (for downstream microservices), password change, and forgot/reset password flows. The domain model is deliberately separated from user profile data, which lives in a separate `core-api`.

---

## 1. Models (`app/models/`)

### `AuthCredential`
- Uses `has_secure_password` (bcrypt). Password digest is never exposed in responses.
- `validates :email` with `URI::MailTo::EMAIL_REGEXP` — good choice over hand-rolled regexes.
- `uniqueness: { case_sensitive: false }` at the model level, but the DB index at `/db/migrate/20240101000002` is a plain `add_index :auth_credentials, :email, unique: true` without `lower()`. This is a race-condition / case-sensitivity gap: two simultaneous registrations for `User@Example.com` and `user@example.com` could both pass the DB unique constraint because the index is case-sensitive at the Postgres level. The Rails normalizer (`normalizes :email`) runs before save, so in practice both would normalize to `user@example.com` before hitting the DB — but the index should still be `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... ON auth_credentials (LOWER(email))` as belt-and-suspenders.
- `after_save :record_password_history, if: :saved_change_to_password_digest?` — correct callback placement; won't fire unless digest actually changed.
- `email_verified_at` column exists but there is no email verification flow in the codebase — no endpoint, no event published, no enforcement. The JWT payload carries `email_verified: false` but nothing gates on it.
- `role` defaults to `'user'`, and the `register_params` strong-parameter permit list at controller line 375 explicitly only permits `[:email, :password]`, which prevents role escalation via mass assignment. Good.

### `RefreshToken`
- Dual timeout strategy (absolute 8-hour expiry + 30-minute idle timeout) is well-designed for a medical app.
- Token generated with `SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(64)` — 512 bits of entropy, effectively unguessable.
- `valid_for_use?` (line 91) checks all three conditions atomically in Ruby. This is fine, but the companion `active` scope (lines 64–68) contains equivalent logic in SQL. The two are not trivially in sync — a future developer changing constants could update one and not the other. Consider using the scope as the basis for `valid_for_use?` by calling it, or extracting constants into a shared helper.
- `touch_last_used!` uses `update!` which triggers validations on every refresh. This is safe but slightly wasteful; `update_column` would skip validations and callbacks for a timestamp-only update. Not a bug, just a note.

### `PasswordResetToken`
- Single-use enforced via `consumed_at` and `valid_for_use?`. Token is consumed before returning success.
- 24-hour expiry is standard; no issues.
- No index on `auth_credential_id` alone (only the composite `token` unique index). Queries that find all tokens for a credential (for cleanup, or listing) will be sequential scans. Unlikely to be a performance issue at small scale.

### `PasswordHistory`
- `password_used?` (line 41) iterates Ruby-side through up to 12 bcrypt records and calls `.is_password?` on each. This is intentionally slow (bcrypt), and for 12 records it's acceptable, but it means every password change triggers up to 12 bcrypt comparisons. This could become a denial-of-service vector if an attacker can spam password-change requests (mitigated in practice by the `authenticate_request` guard on `change_password`).
- `cleanup_old_passwords` uses `destroy_all` instead of `delete_all`. Each record triggers a callback chain unnecessarily; `delete_all` is sufficient here since `PasswordHistory` has no meaningful callbacks.

### `ApplicationRecord`
- Correct minimal base class. Nothing to flag.

---

## 2. Controllers (`app/controllers/`)

### `AuthenticationController` (280 lines of public surface + ~130 private)

**Strong parameters:**
- `register_params` (line 375) correctly limits to `[:email, :password]`, preventing role injection.
- `login`, `logout`, `refresh`, `validate`, `me`, `change_password`, and `forgot_password` all read directly from `params` without a permit block. This is intentional in API-only Rails (no CSRF, no mass assignment to AR models from raw params), but it's worth noting that params like `params[:email]` in `login` (line 89) and `forgot_password` (line 262) are accessed without strong params. No security issue because they're not passed directly to AR `.new` or `.update`, but it's inconsistent style.

**`authenticate_request` (lines 384–400):**
- Correctly extracts the token from the `Authorization: Bearer` header.
- After JWT decode success, does a live DB lookup: `AuthCredential.find_by(id: result.payload[:sub])` (line 395). This means the `validate` endpoint (used by other microservices) does NOT do a DB lookup — it only validates the JWT signature and claims. Consistent with the stateless design intent for inter-service validation.

**`logout` (lines 123–134):**
- Correct ownership check: `refresh_token&.auth_credential_id == @current_user.id` (line 128). Prevents users from revoking tokens belonging to other users.
- Only revokes the single presented refresh token, not all tokens. This is intentional (single-device logout). `change_password` and `reset_password` do revoke all tokens, which is the right escalation.

**`change_password` (lines 240–246):**
- Control flow: checks current password validity → checks confirmation match → attempts update → revokes tokens. The token revocation happens inside `render_password_update_success` (line 330), which is called only if `update_password` returns truthy. However, the `render` call and the `find_each(&:revoke!)` are in the same method, and both happen in the same DB transaction implicitly — but there's no explicit transaction wrapping "update password + revoke tokens". If `revoke!` fails on one token, the password is already changed. Low risk in practice but not atomic.

**`reset_password` (lines 293–305):**
- Same atomicity concern: `finalize_password_reset` calls `consume!` then `find_each(&:revoke!)`. If the connection drops between the two, a partially consumed token could coexist with active refresh tokens. Wrapping `finalize_password_reset` in a transaction would eliminate this risk.

**`forgot_password` (lines 261–269):**
- Correctly returns 200 regardless of whether the email exists.
- No limit on how many `PasswordResetToken` records a single credential can accumulate. A user who clicks "forgot password" 1000 times gets 1000 active tokens until they expire or are cleaned up. The rate limiter (5/hour per IP + 5/hour per email) is the only guard. The existing tokens should probably be invalidated/replaced on a new request, or a maximum of 1 active token per credential should be enforced.
- `EventPublisher.publish` failure (RabbitMQ down) is caught and returns `false` silently. The user never knows the email wasn't sent, and no retry mechanism exists. This is noted in the service design but has production implications.

**Naming note:**
The module is declared as `module API` (uppercase, line 20) but the route namespace is `namespace :api` (lowercase, line 36 of routes.rb), which Rails conventions would resolve to `Api::V1`. This mismatch between `module API::V1` vs `module Api::V1` is a latent issue. It works in practice because Rails' autoloader (`zeitwerk`) maps file paths to constants, and the file lives at `app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, which Zeitwerk loads as `Api::V1::AuthenticationController`. The `module API` declaration in the file creates a different constant (`API::V1::AuthenticationController`). This is a genuine bug — it works only because Rails routes resolve to the class found by Zeitwerk from the path, not the constant declared with `module`. It would silently fail if you ever try to reference `API::V1::AuthenticationController` directly. The file should use `module Api` to match Zeitwerk's expectation.

**`HealthController`:**
- Unauthenticated, fast, returns JSON. No issues. One minor point: it does not check DB connectivity — a process that's alive but disconnected from Postgres would still return 200.

---

## 3. Services (`app/services/`)

### `JWTService`
- Algorithm: HS256 (symmetric). This is appropriate for a closed microservice cluster where all services share the secret. Would need to change to RS256 if any external parties need to verify tokens without access to the secret.
- `secret_key` (lines 116–126): In non-production environments, falls back to `'development-secret-key-change-in-production'`. This is hardcoded in source, meaning if the env var is missing in staging/CI, a predictable secret is used silently. Better to also raise in staging, or log a warning.
- `decode_options` (lines 143–149) verifies `exp`, `iat`, and `iss`. Notably absent: `verify_jti` (JWT ID for replay prevention). Not strictly required here since access tokens are short-lived (15 min) and stateless, but JTI would allow token revocation before expiry (e.g., on logout, instead of relying solely on refresh token revocation).
- The `Result` struct using `Data.define` (Ruby 3.2+) is clean. `payload` and `error` default to `nil`, which means a success result will have `result.error == nil` and a failure result will have `result.payload == nil` — callers must check `success?` first or risk nil errors.

### `EventPublisher`
- Creates a new Bunny connection and channel on every `publish` call (lines 53–58). This is a per-request connection open/close cycle — fine for low volume, but inefficient and fragile under load. Connection pooling or a persistent connection with reconnect logic would be production-grade.
- `automatically_recover: false` (line 74). Combined with no retry logic, a transient RabbitMQ blip silently drops the email event. Acceptable if HIBP-style reliability is not required, but the user has no feedback.
- `log_success` (line 82) logs `payload[:payload][:to]` — the recipient's email address — to Rails logger at INFO level. If logs are aggregated to a third-party service (e.g., Datadog, Papertrail), this leaks email addresses into log infrastructure. Should either not log it, or log at DEBUG level.

### `PasswordBreachChecker`
- k-anonymity HIBP implementation is correct and respects user privacy.
- `REQUEST_TIMEOUT = 5` seconds. This means every registration and password change waits up to 5 seconds if HIBP is slow. This is a synchronous HTTP call on the request thread. Should be in a background job or at minimum have a shorter timeout with a clear timeout error logged.
- On any error (including timeout), returns `false` (safe default — allows password). This is a documented intentional design choice.

### `StringDistance`
- Standard two-row Levenshtein. Memory efficient. No issues.
- Used only for email-similarity check in `PasswordValidator`. The `rubocop:disable` hints at the complexity metric concern — it's a complex function for what it does, but it's correct.

### `PasswordValidator`
- `@common_passwords` is an instance variable on the validator (line 88). Because Rails validators are instantiated once per model class (or per validation call depending on version), this could be shared or not shared across requests. In practice `validates_with ::PasswordValidator` creates a new validator instance each time, so `@common_passwords` is loaded fresh each time — but this is inefficient (file read on every validation). Should be memoized at the class level or loaded once at startup (e.g., as a class-level constant or thread-safe `@@common_passwords`).
- `validate_not_breached` calls the synchronous HIBP API inline. See the EventPublisher concern above — this blocks the request thread for up to 5 seconds.
- Order of validations: length → common → email-similarity → breach → reuse. Breach check runs even if password is too short. Minor: the length check should short-circuit and skip expensive checks. Currently, if length fails, subsequent checks still run because there's no `return` after `validate_length`. Only the breach and reuse checks have explicit `return` guards.

---

## 4. Routes (`config/routes.rb`)

- 9 endpoints under `/api/v1/auth/`. Structure is clear and versioned.
- Rswag mounted at `/api-docs` with both `Rswag::Ui::Engine` and `Rswag::Api::Engine` mounted to the same path (lines 15–16). This is correct for rswag (the API engine serves JSON, the UI serves HTML from the same base path).
- Health check at `/up` is outside the API namespace, which is correct — infrastructure tooling shouldn't need to know the API version.
- No admin namespace. No webhook routes. No internal/service-to-service routes beyond `validate`.
- The `validate` endpoint is `POST` with no auth guard — any caller with the token can validate it. This is by design (the JWT's signature is the auth), but it means the endpoint is unauthenticated in the sense that you don't need to be a registered client to call it. In a zero-trust architecture, you might want IP allowlisting at the load balancer level for this endpoint.

---

## 5. Security Configuration (`config/initializers/`)

### `rack_attack.rb`
- Redis-backed (distributed rate limiting). Correct.
- Login throttled 5/min per IP and 5/min per email. Good defense-in-depth.
- Registration throttled 3/hour per IP. Reasonable.
- Password reset: 5/hour per IP + 5/hour per email, plus 10/hour per IP on the token consumption endpoint. Thorough.
- **Critical gap:** The localhost safelist (lines 32–34) unconditionally allows unlimited requests from `127.0.0.1` and `::1`. In containerized deployments behind a reverse proxy, if the proxy doesn't correctly set `X-Forwarded-For`, all requests may appear to come from `127.0.0.1`, bypassing all rate limiting entirely. The comment says "remove or restrict in production if needed" but this is a hard requirement for production correctness.
- No `blocklist` rules for known-bad IPs or user-agents.
- The `throttled_responder` returns `Retry-After` header with correct calculation (line 122).

### `cors.rb`
- Correctly raises in production if `CORS_ORIGINS` is not set.
- Development fallback is localhost origins only (not `*`). Good.
- Exposes the `Authorization` header to JavaScript (line 51) — necessary for JWT-based auth but means the token is accessible to JavaScript (as designed, since there are no HttpOnly cookies here).
- `max_age: 600` for preflight cache.

### `security_headers.rb`
- `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`, `Permissions-Policy` — all appropriate.
- HSTS header is `nil` in non-production (filtered out by `.compact` on line 28). Correct.
- No `Content-Security-Policy` — not needed for an API-only service with no HTML.

### `filter_parameter_logging.rb`
- Filters `passw`, `email`, `secret`, `token`, `_key`, `crypt`, `salt`, `certificate`, `otp`, `ssn`. Comprehensive. Note that `email` being filtered means email addresses won't appear in request logs — good for privacy, but can make debugging harder. The filter is a substring match so `email` would also filter any param containing "email".

---

## 6. Tests (`spec/`)

### Coverage
- `spec/requests/api/v1/authentication_spec.rb` — comprehensive request-level integration tests for all 9 endpoints + health check. Every endpoint has happy path, common error cases, and security edge cases (cross-user token usage, enumeration prevention, expired token behavior).
- `spec/models/auth_credential_spec.rb` — validates all validations, associations, normalization, authentication, and instance methods.
- `spec/models/refresh_token_spec.rb` — covers token lifecycle states.
- `spec/services/jwt_service_spec.rb` — encodes, decodes, expired, tampered, wrong issuer, malformed.
- `spec/services/event_publisher_spec.rb` — uses instance doubles correctly.
- `spec/tasks/tokens_rake_spec.rb` — rake task cleanup with edge cases.
- `spec/integration/authentication_spec.rb` — rswag integration tests that double as OpenAPI documentation.

### Gaps
- No spec for `PasswordValidator`, `PasswordBreachChecker`, or `StringDistance` in isolation.
- No spec for `PasswordHistory` model.
- No spec for `PasswordResetToken` model (beyond factory usage in request specs).
- `spec/models/password_reset_token_spec.rb` exists (in the file listing) but was not generated with content in the visible files — worth confirming its existence and coverage.
- No tests covering the `PasswordBreachChecker` returning `true` (a breached password being rejected at validation time), since `breached?` is globally stubbed to `false` in `rails_helper.rb` line 73.
- No tests for concurrent registration (the email uniqueness race condition mentioned above).
- No tests for the `EventPublisher` failure path in the context of `forgot_password` (does the endpoint still return 200 if RabbitMQ is down?).

### Test infrastructure
- `BCrypt::Engine.cost = BCrypt::Engine::MIN_COST` in `rails_helper.rb` line 43 — essential for test speed.
- `DatabaseCleaner` with transaction strategy. `use_transactional_fixtures = true` AND `DatabaseCleaner.cleaning` are both active — this is redundant and doubles the overhead. The `config.use_transactional_fixtures = true` and the `DatabaseCleaner` `around` block both wrap tests in transactions. Using one or the other is the standard approach. The DatabaseCleaner `clean_with(:truncation)` before suite is correct, but the `around` block at line 91–95 is unnecessary alongside `use_transactional_fixtures`.
- External HTTP calls (HIBP) stubbed globally. Good.
- No RabbitMQ calls reach real infrastructure (mocked in event_publisher_spec, and not called in other tests because `EventPublisher.publish` rescues all errors silently).
- `freeze_time` and `travel` used correctly for token expiry tests.

---

## 7. Gemfile Dependencies

| Gem | Version | Notes |
|-----|---------|-------|
| `rails` | `~> 8.0.0` | Latest stable. API-only mode. |
| `pg` | `~> 1.5` | Correct for PostgreSQL with UUID support. |
| `bcrypt` | `~> 3.1` | Industry-standard password hashing. |
| `jwt` | `~> 2.9` | Recent version; verify it's the latest for security patches. |
| `rack-attack` | `~> 6.7` | Current. |
| `rack-cors` | `~> 2.0` | Current. |
| `bunny` | `~> 2.22` | RabbitMQ client. No version pin to minor — could pick up breaking changes. |
| `redis` | `~> 4.0` | Note: Redis gem `~> 4.0` is the older client. The current major is 5.x. The `4.x` series is still maintained but `5.x` has improved connection handling. |
| `rswag-api/ui/specs` | `~> 2.16` | Current. |
| `ostruct` | unbounded | Required for rswag compatibility with Ruby 3.5 deprecation. Smart. |
| `puma` | `>= 5.0` | No upper bound — could pick up a future major version. Consider `~> 6.0`. |

**Missing gems worth considering:**
- No `dotenv-rails` — environment variables must be configured externally. Fine for Docker/K8s but adds friction in local dev.
- No `sidekiq` or `good_job` — all work is synchronous. The HIBP check and RabbitMQ publish both happen inline on the request thread.
- No `sentry-ruby` or equivalent error tracking.
- No `strong_migrations` — migrations run without safety checks (safe for this schema size, but worth adding if the DB grows).

---

## 8. Concerns: Security, Architecture, Code Quality, Missing Pieces

### Security

**S1 — Localhost safelist bypasses all rate limiting in containerized deployments.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/config/initializers/rack_attack.rb`, lines 32–34.
If a reverse proxy (nginx, AWS ALB) sits in front and the app sees the proxy's internal IP as `127.0.0.1`, every request is safelisted. This must be explicitly verified and either removed or scoped to a known internal CIDR.

**S2 — Password reset tokens are not invalidated when a new reset is requested.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, lines 261–269.
An attacker who intercepts an old reset email can use it even after the user has requested a new one. On `forgot_password`, any existing active `PasswordResetToken` for the credential should be consumed/invalidated before creating a new one.

**S3 — No active access token revocation.**
When `change_password` or `reset_password` revokes refresh tokens, outstanding access tokens remain valid for up to 15 minutes. For a medical app, this window may be too long. JTI-based revocation with a Redis denylist would close this gap.

**S4 — Sensitive data in logs.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/services/event_publisher.rb`, line 82.
`payload[:payload][:to]` (email address) is logged at INFO level on every successful password reset event publish.

**S5 — Module naming mismatch (`API` vs `Api`).**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, line 20.
`module API` should be `module Api` to match Zeitwerk's constant resolution from the file path. While this works in practice because Rails routing resolves controllers by file path, it's a latent naming inconsistency that could cause hard-to-diagnose issues.

**S6 — `force_ssl` and `assume_ssl` are opt-in via env vars.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/config/environments/production.rb`, lines 64–65.
Neither is enabled by default in production. If these env vars are not set (e.g., in a misconfigured deploy), HTTPS is not enforced at the Rails layer. This should be the secure default, not opt-in.

### Architecture

**A1 — Synchronous HIBP API call on request thread.**
Every registration and password change blocks for up to 5 seconds waiting for an external HTTP call. This should be moved to a background job, or at minimum the timeout should be reduced to ~1 second with a clear fallback.

**A2 — Per-request RabbitMQ connection.**
`EventPublisher` opens and closes a TCP connection to RabbitMQ on every `forgot_password` request. Use a connection pool or a persistent connection with auto-reconnect for production loads.

**A3 — No email verification flow.**
The `email_verified_at` column exists and `email_verified?` is in the JWT payload, but there is no endpoint to verify an email, no event published on registration to trigger a verification email, and no enforcement of verification before any action. This is either a planned-but-unimplemented feature or dead code. Either implement it or remove the column and the JWT claim.

**A4 — No explicit transaction wrapping critical multi-step operations.**
`finalize_password_reset` (token consume + refresh token revocation) and `render_password_update_success` (password update + refresh token revocation) are not wrapped in database transactions. Partial failures leave the system in inconsistent states.

**A5 — No account lockout after repeated failed logins.**
Rate limiting (5 attempts/min) is the only defense against brute-force. NIST SP 800-63B recommends also considering lockout or increasing delay after N failures. The current setup lets an attacker try 5 passwords per minute indefinitely — that's 7,200 attempts/day from one IP.

### Code Quality

**Q1 — `PasswordValidator#common_password?` memoizes at instance level.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/validators/password_validator.rb`, lines 87–96.
`@common_passwords` is an instance-level memoization but the validator is re-instantiated on each call, so it's not actually memoized across calls. The file is read on every validation. Promote to a class-level constant loaded at startup.

**Q2 — `validate_length` does not short-circuit.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/validators/password_validator.rb`, lines 33–40.
After adding a length error, the method returns but the caller (`validate`) continues to run `validate_not_common`, `validate_not_similar_to_email`, and `validate_not_breached` on a password that's already known invalid. Add an early return after `validate_length` if errors were added.

**Q3 — Redundant test database cleaning.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/spec/rails_helper.rb`, lines 82–95.
Both `use_transactional_fixtures = true` and `DatabaseCleaner.cleaning` are active. Remove one.

**Q4 — Double database write on token cleanup could use `delete_all`.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/models/password_history.rb`, line 57.
`destroy_all` fires callbacks per record; `delete_all` is sufficient since no callbacks need to run on password history cleanup.

### Missing Pieces

- **No email verification flow** despite the schema column existing.
- **No account lockout** mechanism.
- **No audit log** — failed login attempts, password changes, and token issuance are not recorded anywhere. Critical for a medical application (HIPAA audit trail requirements).
- **No `Content-Security-Policy`** — not needed for pure API, but the Swagger UI (`/api-docs`) renders HTML and would benefit from one.
- **No readiness vs. liveness distinction** at `/up` — a single health endpoint doesn't distinguish "process is running" from "process can serve traffic" (e.g., DB connected).
- **No token rotation on refresh** — the refresh token is reused across multiple refresh calls. Token rotation (issue a new refresh token on each use and invalidate the old one) would detect token theft by detecting double-use.
- **No `robots.txt`** — minor for an API, but crawlers hitting `/api-docs` could expose the API surface publicly.
- **`RABBITMQ_ENABLED` defaults to `'true'`** — in a fresh environment without RabbitMQ configured, every `forgot_password` will log an error silently. The default should probably be `'false'` so the system works without RabbitMQ configured, and production explicitly sets it to `'true'`.

Now I have a complete picture of the codebase. Here is the full technical assessment.

---

# Technical Assessment: auth-api Rails Service

## Overview

A Rails 8.0 API-only authentication microservice for what appears to be a medical/healthcare platform ("ThatCookiesMine" is mentioned in password reset email copy, but comments reference "medical app requirements" and "clinical settings"). It handles registration, login, logout, token refresh, token validation (for downstream microservices), password change, and forgot/reset password flows. The domain model is deliberately separated from user profile data, which lives in a separate `core-api`.

---

## 1. Models (`app/models/`)

### `AuthCredential`
- Uses `has_secure_password` (bcrypt). Password digest is never exposed in responses.
- `validates :email` with `URI::MailTo::EMAIL_REGEXP` — good choice over hand-rolled regexes.
- `uniqueness: { case_sensitive: false }` at the model level, but the DB index at `/db/migrate/20240101000002` is a plain `add_index :auth_credentials, :email, unique: true` without `lower()`. This is a race-condition / case-sensitivity gap: two simultaneous registrations for `User@Example.com` and `user@example.com` could both pass the DB unique constraint because the index is case-sensitive at the Postgres level. The Rails normalizer (`normalizes :email`) runs before save, so in practice both would normalize to `user@example.com` before hitting the DB — but the index should still be `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... ON auth_credentials (LOWER(email))` as belt-and-suspenders.
- `after_save :record_password_history, if: :saved_change_to_password_digest?` — correct callback placement; won't fire unless digest actually changed.
- `email_verified_at` column exists but there is no email verification flow in the codebase — no endpoint, no event published, no enforcement. The JWT payload carries `email_verified: false` but nothing gates on it.
- `role` defaults to `'user'`, and the `register_params` strong-parameter permit list at controller line 375 explicitly only permits `[:email, :password]`, which prevents role escalation via mass assignment. Good.

### `RefreshToken`
- Dual timeout strategy (absolute 8-hour expiry + 30-minute idle timeout) is well-designed for a medical app.
- Token generated with `SecureRandom.urlsafe_base64(64)` — 512 bits of entropy, effectively unguessable.
- `valid_for_use?` (line 91) checks all three conditions atomically in Ruby. This is fine, but the companion `active` scope (lines 64–68) contains equivalent logic in SQL. The two are not trivially in sync — a future developer changing constants could update one and not the other. Consider using the scope as the basis for `valid_for_use?` by calling it, or extracting constants into a shared helper.
- `touch_last_used!` uses `update!` which triggers validations on every refresh. This is safe but slightly wasteful; `update_column` would skip validations and callbacks for a timestamp-only update. Not a bug, just a note.

### `PasswordResetToken`
- Single-use enforced via `consumed_at` and `valid_for_use?`. Token is consumed before returning success.
- 24-hour expiry is standard; no issues.
- No index on `auth_credential_id` alone (only the composite `token` unique index). Queries that find all tokens for a credential (for cleanup, or listing) will be sequential scans. Unlikely to be a performance issue at small scale.

### `PasswordHistory`
- `password_used?` (line 41) iterates Ruby-side through up to 12 bcrypt records and calls `.is_password?` on each. This is intentionally slow (bcrypt), and for 12 records it's acceptable, but it means every password change triggers up to 12 bcrypt comparisons. This could become a denial-of-service vector if an attacker can spam password-change requests (mitigated in practice by the `authenticate_request` guard on `change_password`).
- `cleanup_old_passwords` uses `destroy_all` instead of `delete_all`. Each record triggers a callback chain unnecessarily; `delete_all` is sufficient here since `PasswordHistory` has no meaningful callbacks.

### `ApplicationRecord`
- Correct minimal base class. Nothing to flag.

---

## 2. Controllers (`app/controllers/`)

### `AuthenticationController` (280 lines of public surface + ~130 private)

**Strong parameters:**
- `register_params` (line 375) correctly limits to `[:email, :password]`, preventing role injection.
- `login`, `logout`, `refresh`, `validate`, `me`, `change_password`, and `forgot_password` all read directly from `params` without a permit block. This is intentional in API-only Rails (no CSRF, no mass assignment to AR models from raw params), but it's worth noting that params like `params[:email]` in `login` (line 89) and `forgot_password` (line 262) are accessed without strong params. No security issue because they're not passed directly to AR `.new` or `.update`, but it's inconsistent style.

**`authenticate_request` (lines 384–400):**
- Correctly extracts the token from the `Authorization: Bearer` header.
- After JWT decode success, does a live DB lookup: `AuthCredential.find_by(id: result.payload[:sub])` (line 395). This means the `validate` endpoint (used by other microservices) does NOT do a DB lookup — it only validates the JWT signature and claims. Consistent with the stateless design intent for inter-service validation.

**`logout` (lines 123–134):**
- Correct ownership check: `refresh_token&.auth_credential_id == @current_user.id` (line 128). Prevents users from revoking tokens belonging to other users.
- Only revokes the single presented refresh token, not all tokens. This is intentional (single-device logout). `change_password` and `reset_password` do revoke all tokens, which is the right escalation.

**`change_password` (lines 240–246):**
- Control flow: checks current password validity → checks confirmation match → attempts update → revokes tokens. The token revocation happens inside `render_password_update_success` (line 330), which is called only if `update_password` returns truthy. However, the `render` call and the `find_each(&:revoke!)` are in the same method, and both happen in the same DB transaction implicitly — but there's no explicit transaction wrapping "update password + revoke tokens". If `revoke!` fails on one token, the password is already changed. Low risk in practice but not atomic.

**`reset_password` (lines 293–305):**
- Same atomicity concern: `finalize_password_reset` calls `consume!` then `find_each(&:revoke!)`. If the connection drops between the two, a partially consumed token could coexist with active refresh tokens. Wrapping `finalize_password_reset` in a transaction would eliminate this risk.

**`forgot_password` (lines 261–269):**
- Correctly returns 200 regardless of whether the email exists.
- No limit on how many `PasswordResetToken` records a single credential can accumulate. A user who clicks "forgot password" 1000 times gets 1000 active tokens until they expire or are cleaned up. The rate limiter (5/hour per IP + 5/hour per email) is the only guard. The existing tokens should probably be invalidated/replaced on a new request, or a maximum of 1 active token per credential should be enforced.
- `EventPublisher.publish` failure (RabbitMQ down) is caught and returns `false` silently. The user never knows the email wasn't sent, and no retry mechanism exists. This is noted in the service design but has production implications.

**Naming note:**
The module is declared as `module API` (uppercase, line 20) but the route namespace is `namespace :api` (lowercase, line 36 of routes.rb), which Rails conventions would resolve to `Api::V1`. This mismatch between `module API::V1` vs `module Api::V1` is a latent issue. It works in practice because Rails' autoloader (`zeitwerk`) maps file paths to constants, and the file lives at `app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, which Zeitwerk loads as `Api::V1::AuthenticationController`. The `module API` declaration in the file creates a different constant (`API::V1::AuthenticationController`). This is a genuine bug — it works only because Rails routes resolve to the class found by Zeitwerk from the path, not the constant declared with `module`. It would silently fail if you ever try to reference `API::V1::AuthenticationController` directly. The file should use `module Api` to match Zeitwerk's expectation.

**`HealthController`:**
- Unauthenticated, fast, returns JSON. No issues. One minor point: it does not check DB connectivity — a process that's alive but disconnected from Postgres would still return 200.

---

## 3. Services (`app/services/`)

### `JWTService`
- Algorithm: HS256 (symmetric). This is appropriate for a closed microservice cluster where all services share the secret. Would need to change to RS256 if any external parties need to verify tokens without access to the secret.
- `secret_key` (lines 116–126): In non-production environments, falls back to `'development-secret-key-change-in-production'`. This is hardcoded in source, meaning if the env var is missing in staging/CI, a predictable secret is used silently. Better to also raise in staging, or log a warning.
- `decode_options` (lines 143–149) verifies `exp`, `iat`, and `iss`. Notably absent: `verify_jti` (JWT ID for replay prevention). Not strictly required here since access tokens are short-lived (15 min) and stateless, but JTI would allow token revocation before expiry (e.g., on logout, instead of relying solely on refresh token revocation).
- The `Result` struct using `Data.define` (Ruby 3.2+) is clean. `payload` and `error` default to `nil`, which means a success result will have `result.error == nil` and a failure result will have `result.payload == nil` — callers must check `success?` first or risk nil errors.

### `EventPublisher`
- Creates a new Bunny connection and channel on every `publish` call (lines 53–58). This is a per-request connection open/close cycle — fine for low volume, but inefficient and fragile under load. Connection pooling or a persistent connection with reconnect logic would be production-grade.
- `automatically_recover: false` (line 74). Combined with no retry logic, a transient RabbitMQ blip silently drops the email event. Acceptable if HIBP-style reliability is not required, but the user has no feedback.
- `log_success` (line 82) logs `payload[:payload][:to]` — the recipient's email address — to Rails logger at INFO level. If logs are aggregated to a third-party service (e.g., Datadog, Papertrail), this leaks email addresses into log infrastructure. Should either not log it, or log at DEBUG level.

### `PasswordBreachChecker`
- k-anonymity HIBP implementation is correct and respects user privacy.
- `REQUEST_TIMEOUT = 5` seconds. This means every registration and password change waits up to 5 seconds if HIBP is slow. This is a synchronous HTTP call on the request thread. Should be in a background job or at minimum have a shorter timeout with a clear timeout error logged.
- On any error (including timeout), returns `false` (safe default — allows password). This is a documented intentional design choice.

### `StringDistance`
- Standard two-row Levenshtein. Memory efficient. No issues.
- Used only for email-similarity check in `PasswordValidator`. The `rubocop:disable` hints at the complexity metric concern — it's a complex function for what it does, but it's correct.

### `PasswordValidator`
- `@common_passwords` is an instance variable on the validator (line 88). Because Rails validators are instantiated once per model class (or per validation call depending on version), this could be shared or not shared across requests. In practice `validates_with ::PasswordValidator` creates a new validator instance each time, so `@common_passwords` is loaded fresh each time — but this is inefficient (file read on every validation). Should be memoized at the class level or loaded once at startup (e.g., as a class-level constant or thread-safe `@@common_passwords`).
- `validate_not_breached` calls the synchronous HIBP API inline. See the EventPublisher concern above — this blocks the request thread for up to 5 seconds.
- Order of validations: length → common → email-similarity → breach → reuse. Breach check runs even if password is too short. Minor: the length check should short-circuit and skip expensive checks. Currently, if length fails, subsequent checks still run because there's no `return` after `validate_length`. Only the breach and reuse checks have explicit `return` guards.

---

## 4. Routes (`config/routes.rb`)

- 9 endpoints under `/api/v1/auth/`. Structure is clear and versioned.
- Rswag mounted at `/api-docs` with both `Rswag::Ui::Engine` and `Rswag::Api::Engine` mounted to the same path (lines 15–16). This is correct for rswag (the API engine serves JSON, the UI serves HTML from the same base path).
- Health check at `/up` is outside the API namespace, which is correct — infrastructure tooling shouldn't need to know the API version.
- No admin namespace. No webhook routes. No internal/service-to-service routes beyond `validate`.
- The `validate` endpoint is `POST` with no auth guard — any caller with the token can validate it. This is by design (the JWT's signature is the auth), but it means the endpoint is unauthenticated in the sense that you don't need to be a registered client to call it. In a zero-trust architecture, you might want IP allowlisting at the load balancer level for this endpoint.

---

## 5. Security Configuration (`config/initializers/`)

### `rack_attack.rb`
- Redis-backed (distributed rate limiting). Correct.
- Login throttled 5/min per IP and 5/min per email. Good defense-in-depth.
- Registration throttled 3/hour per IP. Reasonable.
- Password reset: 5/hour per IP + 5/hour per email, plus 10/hour per IP on the token consumption endpoint. Thorough.
- **Critical gap:** The localhost safelist (lines 32–34) unconditionally allows unlimited requests from `127.0.0.1` and `::1`. In containerized deployments behind a reverse proxy, if the proxy doesn't correctly set `X-Forwarded-For`, all requests may appear to come from `127.0.0.1`, bypassing all rate limiting entirely. The comment says "remove or restrict in production if needed" but this is a hard requirement for production correctness.
- No `blocklist` rules for known-bad IPs or user-agents.
- The `throttled_responder` returns `Retry-After` header with correct calculation (line 122).

### `cors.rb`
- Correctly raises in production if `CORS_ORIGINS` is not set.
- Development fallback is localhost origins only (not `*`). Good.
- Exposes the `Authorization` header to JavaScript (line 51) — necessary for JWT-based auth but means the token is accessible to JavaScript (as designed, since there are no HttpOnly cookies here).
- `max_age: 600` for preflight cache.

### `security_headers.rb`
- `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, `Referrer-Policy: no-referrer`, `Permissions-Policy` — all appropriate.
- HSTS header is `nil` in non-production (filtered out by `.compact` on line 28). Correct.
- No `Content-Security-Policy` — not needed for an API-only service with no HTML.

### `filter_parameter_logging.rb`
- Filters `passw`, `email`, `secret`, `token`, `_key`, `crypt`, `salt`, `certificate`, `otp`, `ssn`. Comprehensive. Note that `email` being filtered means email addresses won't appear in request logs — good for privacy, but can make debugging harder. The filter is a substring match so `email` would also filter any param containing "email".

---

## 6. Tests (`spec/`)

### Coverage
- `spec/requests/api/v1/authentication_spec.rb` — comprehensive request-level integration tests for all 9 endpoints + health check. Every endpoint has happy path, common error cases, and security edge cases (cross-user token usage, enumeration prevention, expired token behavior).
- `spec/models/auth_credential_spec.rb` — validates all validations, associations, normalization, authentication, and instance methods.
- `spec/models/refresh_token_spec.rb` — covers token lifecycle states.
- `spec/services/jwt_service_spec.rb` — encodes, decodes, expired, tampered, wrong issuer, malformed.
- `spec/services/event_publisher_spec.rb` — uses instance doubles correctly.
- `spec/tasks/tokens_rake_spec.rb` — rake task cleanup with edge cases.
- `spec/integration/authentication_spec.rb` — rswag integration tests that double as OpenAPI documentation.

### Gaps
- No spec for `PasswordValidator`, `PasswordBreachChecker`, or `StringDistance` in isolation.
- No spec for `PasswordHistory` model.
- No spec for `PasswordResetToken` model (beyond factory usage in request specs).
- `spec/models/password_reset_token_spec.rb` exists (in the file listing) but was not generated with content in the visible files — worth confirming its existence and coverage.
- No tests covering the `PasswordBreachChecker` returning `true` (a breached password being rejected at validation time), since `breached?` is globally stubbed to `false` in `rails_helper.rb` line 73.
- No tests for concurrent registration (the email uniqueness race condition mentioned above).
- No tests for the `EventPublisher` failure path in the context of `forgot_password` (does the endpoint still return 200 if RabbitMQ is down?).

### Test infrastructure
- `BCrypt::Engine.cost = BCrypt::Engine::MIN_COST` in `rails_helper.rb` line 43 — essential for test speed.
- `DatabaseCleaner` with transaction strategy. `use_transactional_fixtures = true` AND `DatabaseCleaner.cleaning` are both active — this is redundant and doubles the overhead. The `config.use_transactional_fixtures = true` and the `DatabaseCleaner` `around` block both wrap tests in transactions. Using one or the other is the standard approach. The DatabaseCleaner `clean_with(:truncation)` before suite is correct, but the `around` block at line 91–95 is unnecessary alongside `use_transactional_fixtures`.
- External HTTP calls (HIBP) stubbed globally. Good.
- No RabbitMQ calls reach real infrastructure (mocked in event_publisher_spec, and not called in other tests because `EventPublisher.publish` rescues all errors silently).
- `freeze_time` and `travel` used correctly for token expiry tests.

---

## 7. Gemfile Dependencies

| Gem | Version | Notes |
|-----|---------|-------|
| `rails` | `~> 8.0.0` | Latest stable. API-only mode. |
| `pg` | `~> 1.5` | Correct for PostgreSQL with UUID support. |
| `bcrypt` | `~> 3.1` | Industry-standard password hashing. |
| `jwt` | `~> 2.9` | Recent version; verify it's the latest for security patches. |
| `rack-attack` | `~> 6.7` | Current. |
| `rack-cors` | `~> 2.0` | Current. |
| `bunny` | `~> 2.22` | RabbitMQ client. No version pin to minor — could pick up breaking changes. |
| `redis` | `~> 4.0` | Note: Redis gem `~> 4.0` is the older client. The current major is 5.x. The `4.x` series is still maintained but `5.x` has improved connection handling. |
| `rswag-api/ui/specs` | `~> 2.16` | Current. |
| `ostruct` | unbounded | Required for rswag compatibility with Ruby 3.5 deprecation. Smart. |
| `puma` | `>= 5.0` | No upper bound — could pick up a future major version. Consider `~> 6.0`. |

**Missing gems worth considering:**
- No `dotenv-rails` — environment variables must be configured externally. Fine for Docker/K8s but adds friction in local dev.
- No `sidekiq` or `good_job` — all work is synchronous. The HIBP check and RabbitMQ publish both happen inline on the request thread.
- No `sentry-ruby` or equivalent error tracking.
- No `strong_migrations` — migrations run without safety checks (safe for this schema size, but worth adding if the DB grows).

---

## 8. Concerns: Security, Architecture, Code Quality, Missing Pieces

### Security

**S1 — Localhost safelist bypasses all rate limiting in containerized deployments.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/config/initializers/rack_attack.rb`, lines 32–34.
If a reverse proxy (nginx, AWS ALB) sits in front and the app sees the proxy's internal IP as `127.0.0.1`, every request is safelisted. This must be explicitly verified and either removed or scoped to a known internal CIDR.

**S2 — Password reset tokens are not invalidated when a new reset is requested.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, lines 261–269.
An attacker who intercepts an old reset email can use it even after the user has requested a new one. On `forgot_password`, any existing active `PasswordResetToken` for the credential should be consumed/invalidated before creating a new one.

**S3 — No active access token revocation.**
When `change_password` or `reset_password` revokes refresh tokens, outstanding access tokens remain valid for up to 15 minutes. For a medical app, this window may be too long. JTI-based revocation with a Redis denylist would close this gap.

**S4 — Sensitive data in logs.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/services/event_publisher.rb`, line 82.
`payload[:payload][:to]` (email address) is logged at INFO level on every successful password reset event publish.

**S5 — Module naming mismatch (`API` vs `Api`).**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/controllers/api/v1/authentication_controller.rb`, line 20.
`module API` should be `module Api` to match Zeitwerk's constant resolution from the file path. While this works in practice because Rails routing resolves controllers by file path, it's a latent naming inconsistency that could cause hard-to-diagnose issues.

**S6 — `force_ssl` and `assume_ssl` are opt-in via env vars.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/config/environments/production.rb`, lines 64–65.
Neither is enabled by default in production. If these env vars are not set (e.g., in a misconfigured deploy), HTTPS is not enforced at the Rails layer. This should be the secure default, not opt-in.

### Architecture

**A1 — Synchronous HIBP API call on request thread.**
Every registration and password change blocks for up to 5 seconds waiting for an external HTTP call. This should be moved to a background job, or at minimum the timeout should be reduced to ~1 second with a clear fallback.

**A2 — Per-request RabbitMQ connection.**
`EventPublisher` opens and closes a TCP connection to RabbitMQ on every `forgot_password` request. Use a connection pool or a persistent connection with auto-reconnect for production loads.

**A3 — No email verification flow.**
The `email_verified_at` column exists and `email_verified?` is in the JWT payload, but there is no endpoint to verify an email, no event published on registration to trigger a verification email, and no enforcement of verification before any action. This is either a planned-but-unimplemented feature or dead code. Either implement it or remove the column and the JWT claim.

**A4 — No explicit transaction wrapping critical multi-step operations.**
`finalize_password_reset` (token consume + refresh token revocation) and `render_password_update_success` (password update + refresh token revocation) are not wrapped in database transactions. Partial failures leave the system in inconsistent states.

**A5 — No account lockout after repeated failed logins.**
Rate limiting (5 attempts/min) is the only defense against brute-force. NIST SP 800-63B recommends also considering lockout or increasing delay after N failures. The current setup lets an attacker try 5 passwords per minute indefinitely — that's 7,200 attempts/day from one IP.

### Code Quality

**Q1 — `PasswordValidator#common_password?` memoizes at instance level.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/validators/password_validator.rb`, lines 87–96.
`@common_passwords` is an instance-level memoization but the validator is re-instantiated on each call, so it's not actually memoized across calls. The file is read on every validation. Promote to a class-level constant loaded at startup.

**Q2 — `validate_length` does not short-circuit.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/validators/password_validator.rb`, lines 33–40.
After adding a length error, the method returns but the caller (`validate`) continues to run `validate_not_common`, `validate_not_similar_to_email`, and `validate_not_breached` on a password that's already known invalid. Add an early return after `validate_length` if errors were added.

**Q3 — Redundant test database cleaning.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/spec/rails_helper.rb`, lines 82–95.
Both `use_transactional_fixtures = true` and `DatabaseCleaner.cleaning` are active. Remove one.

**Q4 — Double database write on token cleanup could use `delete_all`.**
File: `/home/tymoyato/work/auth-api/app/models/password_history.rb`, line 57.
`destroy_all` fires callbacks per record; `delete_all` is sufficient since no callbacks need to run on password history cleanup.

### Missing Pieces

- **No email verification flow** despite the schema column existing.
- **No account lockout** mechanism.
- **No audit log** — failed login attempts, password changes, and token issuance are not recorded anywhere. Critical for a medical application (HIPAA audit trail requirements).
- **No `Content-Security-Policy`** — not needed for pure API, but the Swagger UI (`/api-docs`) renders HTML and would benefit from one.
- **No readiness vs. liveness distinction** at `/up` — a single health endpoint doesn't distinguish "process is running" from "process can serve traffic" (e.g., DB connected).
- **No token rotation on refresh** — the refresh token is reused across multiple refresh calls. Token rotation (issue a new refresh token on each use and invalidate the old one) would detect token theft by detecting double-use.
- **No `robots.txt`** — minor for an API, but crawlers hitting `/api-docs` could expose the API surface publicly.
- **`RABBITMQ_ENABLED` defaults to `'true'`** — in a fresh environment without RabbitMQ configured, every `forgot_password` will log an error silently. The default should probably be `'false'` so the system works without RabbitMQ configured, and production explicitly sets it to `'true'`.

content.text:
Solid foundation, real gaps worth fixing. Summary:

---

## Strengths

- NIST 800-63B compliance (length, common passwords, HIBP, email similarity, history)
- Dual-timeout refresh tokens (absolute 8h + idle 30min) — right for medical context
- Correct enumeration prevention on `forgot_password`
- Redis-backed distributed rate limiting
- Strong param guards prevent role escalation
- Comprehensive request-level test suite

---

## Critical Issues

**S1 — Rack-Attack localhost safelist** (`config/initializers/rack_attack.rb:32-34`)
Behind a reverse proxy, all requests may appear as `127.0.0.1` → rate limiting fully bypassed. Must verify in production.

**S2 — Old reset tokens not invalidated** (`authentication_controller.rb:261-269`)
New `forgot_password` request doesn't invalidate prior active tokens. Attacker with old email link can still use it.

**S3 — No access token revocation on logout/password change**
Refresh tokens revoked, but 15-min access tokens stay valid. Too long for medical app. JTI denylist in Redis would close this.

**A4 — No DB transactions on critical operations**
`finalize_password_reset` (consume token + revoke refresh tokens) not atomic. Partial failure leaves inconsistent state.

---

## Architecture Problems

**A1 — HIBP check is synchronous on request thread** — blocks up to 5s on every registration/password change. Move to background job.

**A2 — Per-request RabbitMQ connection** in `EventPublisher` — opens/closes TCP connection every `forgot_password`. Use connection pool.

**A3 — Dead `email_verified_at` column** — schema has it, JWT payload includes it, but zero code enforces or populates it. Implement or delete.

**A5 — No account lockout** — rate limit (5/min) only. 7,200 brute-force attempts/day per IP slip through indefinitely.

---

## Smaller Issues

| # | Location | Issue |
|---|----------|-------|
| Q1 | `password_validator.rb:88` | `@common_passwords` re-reads file every validation — should be class-level constant |
| Q2 | `password_validator.rb:33` | Length check doesn't short-circuit — HIBP still called on 3-char passwords |
| Q3 | `rails_helper.rb:82-95` | Both `use_transactional_fixtures` and `DatabaseCleaner` active — redundant |
| Q4 | `password_history.rb:57` | `destroy_all` → use `delete_all`, no callbacks needed |
| S5 | `authentication_controller.rb:20` | `module API` should be `module Api` to match Zeitwerk path resolution |
| S4 | `event_publisher.rb:82` | Email address logged at INFO — leaks to log aggregators |

---

## Missing Entirely

- **Audit log** — no record of login attempts, token issuance, or password changes. HIPAA problem.
- **Refresh token rotation** — same token reused; no theft detection via double-use detection.
- **`force_ssl` is opt-in** in production config — misconfigured deploy = no HTTPS enforcement.
- **`PasswordResetToken` accumulation** — no limit on active tokens per credential; spam `forgot_password` 1000x = 1000 valid tokens.

---

Overall: good security instincts, shaky operational hardening. The top 3 to fix first: S1 (rack-attack bypass), A4 (missing transactions), S2 (old reset tokens).

# frozen_string_literal: true

# =============================================================================
# RACK::ATTACK - Rate Limiting & Brute-Force Protection
# =============================================================================
# This middleware protects against:
# 1. Brute-force login attacks (trying many passwords)
# 2. Credential stuffing (trying leaked username/password combos)
# 3. API abuse (too many requests from one client)
#
# OWASP Recommendation: Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints

module Rack
  class Attack
    # ===========================================================================
    # CONFIGURATION
    # ===========================================================================

    # Use Redis for distributed rate limiting across server instances.
    # MemoryStore doesn't work in multi-instance deployments (each instance has
    # separate counters) and resets on restart.
    # Note: Using Redis.new directly avoids a connection_pool 3.x incompatibility
    # with ActiveSupport::Cache::RedisCacheStore in Rails 8.
    Rack::Attack.cache.store = Redis.new(url: ENV.fetch('REDIS_URL', 'redis://localhost:6379/0'))

    # ===========================================================================
    # SAFELIST - Always allow these requests (skip rate limiting)
    # ===========================================================================

    # Allow unlimited requests from localhost (development convenience)
    # SECURITY: Remove or restrict this in production if needed
    safelist('allow-localhost') do |req|
      ['127.0.0.1', '::1'].include?(req.ip)
    end

    # ===========================================================================
    # THROTTLE: LOGIN ATTEMPTS
    # ===========================================================================
    # Limit login attempts to prevent brute-force password guessing.
    #
    # Strategy: Limit by IP AND by email (defense in depth)
    # - IP limit: Stops attacker using one machine to try many accounts
    # - Email limit: Stops distributed attack targeting one account

    # Limit: 5 login attempts per IP per minute
    # After 5 failed logins from same IP, block for 1 minute
    throttle('logins/ip', limit: 5, period: 1.minute) do |req|
      req.ip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/login' && req.post?
    end

    # Limit: 5 login attempts per email per minute
    # Protects specific accounts from targeted attacks
    throttle('logins/email', limit: 5, period: 1.minute) do |req|
      if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/login' && req.post?
        # Normalize email: downcase and strip whitespace
        # req.params['email'] might be nil, hence &.
        req.params['email']&.to_s&.downcase&.strip
      end
    end

    # ===========================================================================
    # THROTTLE: REGISTRATION
    # ===========================================================================
    # Limit account creation to prevent:
    # - Spam account creation
    # - Resource exhaustion attacks

    # Limit: 3 registrations per IP per hour
    throttle('registrations/ip', limit: 3, period: 1.hour) do |req|
      req.ip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/register' && req.post?
    end

    # ===========================================================================
    # THROTTLE: PASSWORD RESET
    # ===========================================================================
    # Limit password reset requests to prevent brute-force token guessing
    # and account enumeration through email-based reset flows.

    # Limit: 5 reset requests per IP per hour
    throttle('password-reset/ip', limit: 5, period: 1.hour) do |req|
      req.ip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/forgot-password' && req.post?
    end

    # Limit: 5 reset attempts per email per hour
    throttle('password-reset/email', limit: 5, period: 1.hour) do |req|
      req.params['email']&.to_s&.downcase&.strip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/forgot-password' && req.post?
    end

    # Limit: 10 token consumption attempts per IP per hour (reset-password endpoint)
    throttle('password-reset-consume/ip', limit: 10, period: 1.hour) do |req|
      req.ip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/reset-password' && req.post?
    end

    # ===========================================================================
    # THROTTLE: TOKEN REFRESH
    # ===========================================================================
    # Limit token refresh to prevent token enumeration attacks

    # Limit: 10 refresh attempts per IP per minute
    throttle('refresh/ip', limit: 10, period: 1.minute) do |req|
      req.ip if req.path == '/api/v1/auth/refresh' && req.post?
    end

    # ===========================================================================
    # THROTTLE: GENERAL API
    # ===========================================================================
    # Limit overall API requests per IP to prevent abuse

    # Limit: 100 requests per IP per minute
    throttle('api/ip', limit: 100, period: 1.minute, &:ip)

    # ===========================================================================
    # BLOCKED RESPONSE
    # ===========================================================================
    # What to return when a request is blocked

    self.throttled_responder = lambda do |request|
      # Calculate when the client can retry
      match_data = request.env['rack.attack.match_data']
      now = match_data[:epoch_time]
      retry_after = match_data[:period] - (now % match_data[:period])

      # Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
      # SECURITY: Generic message, don't reveal rate limit details
      [
        429,
        {
          'Content-Type' => 'application/json',
          'Retry-After' => retry_after.to_s
        },
        [{ error: 'Too many requests. Please try again later.' }.to_json]
      ]
    end
  end
end

3	# =============================================================================
4	# AUTHENTICATION CONTROLLER
5	# =============================================================================
6	# Handles all authentication operations:
7	# - register: Create new user account
8	# - login: Authenticate and issue tokens
9	# - logout: Revoke refresh token
10	# - refresh: Exchange refresh token for new access token
11	# - validate: Verify access token (for other microservices)
12	# - me: Get current user info
13	#
14	# SECURITY PRINCIPLES:
15	# 1. Generic error messages (don't reveal if email exists)
16	# 2. Timing-safe comparisons (bcrypt handles this)
17	# 3. Rate limiting (handled by Rack::Attack middleware)
18	# 4. Token validation on protected endpoints

21	  module V1
22	    class AuthenticationController < ApplicationController
23	      # ========================================================================
24	      # BEFORE ACTIONS
25	      # ========================================================================
26	      # Callbacks that run before specific actions
27	
28	      # Require authentication for these actions
29	      # User must provide valid access token in Authorization header
30	      before_action :authenticate_request, only: %i[me logout change_password]
31	
32	      # ========================================================================
33	      # ACTIONS
34	      # ========================================================================
35	
36	      # POST /api/v1/auth/register
37	      # Create a new user account
38	      #
39	      # Request body:
40	      #   { "email": "user@example.com", "password": "securepassword" }
41	      #
42	      # Success response (201 Created):
43	      #   {
44	      #     "message": "Account created successfully",
45	      #     "user": { "id": "...", "email": "...", ... },
46	      #     "access_token": "eyJ...",
47	      #     "refresh_token": "abc123..."
48	      #   }
49	      #
50	      # Error response (422 Unprocessable Entity):
51	      #   { "errors": ["Email has already been taken", "Password is too short"] }
52	      def register
53	        credential = AuthCredential.new(register_params)
54	
55	        if credential.save
56	          tokens = generate_tokens(credential)
57	          render json: {
58	            message: 'Account created successfully',
59	            user: user_payload(credential),
60	            access_token: tokens[:access_token],
61	            refresh_token: tokens[:refresh_token]
62	          }, status: :created
63	        else
64	          render json: { errors: credential.errors.full_messages }, status: :unprocessable_content
65	        end
66	      end
67	
68	      # POST /api/v1/auth/login
69	      # Authenticate user and issue tokens
70	      #
71	      # Request body:
72	      #   { "email": "user@example.com", "password": "securepassword" }
73	      #
74	      # Success response (200 OK):
75	      #   {
76	      #     "message": "Login successful",
77	      #     "user": { ... },
78	      #     "access_token": "eyJ...",
79	      #     "refresh_token": "abc123..."
80	      #   }
81	      #
82	      # Error response (401 Unauthorized):
83	      #   { "error": "Invalid email or password" }
84	      #
85	      # SECURITY: Same error for wrong email OR wrong password
86	      # This prevents attackers from discovering valid emails
87	      def login
88	        # Find user by email (already normalized by model)
89	        credential = AuthCredential.find_by(email: params[:email]&.downcase&.strip)
90	
91	        # Authenticate: check if password matches
92	        # authenticate() returns the credential if password matches, false otherwise
93	        # SECURITY: bcrypt.authenticate is timing-safe (constant time comparison)
94	        if credential&.authenticate(params[:password])
95	          tokens = generate_tokens(credential)
96	          render json: {
97	            message: 'Login successful',
98	            user: user_payload(credential),
99	            access_token: tokens[:access_token],
100	            refresh_token: tokens[:refresh_token]
101	          }
102	        else
103	          # SECURITY: Generic error message
104	          # Don't reveal whether the email exists or the password was wrong
105	          render json: { error: 'Invalid email or password' }, status: :unauthorized
106	        end
107	      end
108	
109	      # POST /api/v1/auth/logout
110	      # Revoke the refresh token (end session)
111	      #
112	      # Headers:
113	      #   Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
114	      #
115	      # Request body:
116	      #   { "refresh_token": "abc123..." }
117	      #
118	      # Success response (200 OK):
119	      #   { "message": "Logged out successfully" }
120	      #
121	      # Error response (422 Unprocessable Entity):
122	      #   { "error": "Invalid refresh token" }
123	      def logout
124	        refresh_token = RefreshToken.find_by(token: params[:refresh_token])
125	
126	        # Verify the refresh token belongs to the current user
127	        # This prevents users from revoking other users' tokens
128	        if refresh_token&.auth_credential_id == @current_user.id
129	          refresh_token.revoke!
130	          render json: { message: 'Logged out successfully' }
131	        else
132	          render json: { error: 'Invalid refresh token' }, status: :unprocessable_content
133	        end
134	      end
135	
136	      # POST /api/v1/auth/refresh
137	      # Exchange refresh token for new access token
138	      #
139	      # Request body:
140	      #   { "refresh_token": "abc123..." }
141	      #
142	      # Success response (200 OK):
143	      #   { "access_token": "eyJ...", "user": { ... } }
144	      #
145	      # Error response (401 Unauthorized):
146	      #   { "error": "Invalid or expired refresh token" }
147	      #
148	      # This endpoint:
149	      # 1. Validates the refresh token (not expired, not revoked, not idle)
150	      # 2. Updates last_used_at (resets idle timeout)
151	      # 3. Issues new access token
152	      def refresh
153	        refresh_token = RefreshToken.find_by(token: params[:refresh_token])
154	
155	        if refresh_token&.valid_for_use?
156	          # Update last_used_at to reset idle timeout
157	          refresh_token.touch_last_used!
158	
159	          credential = refresh_token.auth_credential
160	          access_token = JWTService.generate_access_token(credential)
161	
162	          render json: {
163	            access_token: access_token,
164	            user: user_payload(credential)
165	          }
166	        else
167	          # SECURITY: Generic error message

169	          render json: { error: 'Invalid or expired refresh token' }, status: :unauthorized
170	        end
171	      end

173	      # POST /api/v1/auth/validate
174	      # Validate an access token (for other microservices)
175	      #
176	      # Headers:
177	      #   Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
178	      #
179	      # Success response (200 OK):
180	      #   { "valid": true, "payload": { "sub": "...", "email": "...", ... } }
181	      #
182	      # Error response (401 Unauthorized):
183	      #   { "valid": false, "error": "Token has expired" }
184	      #
185	      # CRITICAL: This endpoint is called by Core API and Admin App
186	      # to verify user tokens before processing requests.
187	      def validate
188	        token = extract_token_from_header
189	
190	        if token.blank?
191	          render json: { valid: false, error: 'No token provided' }, status: :unauthorized
192	          return
193	        end
194

196	
197	        if result.success?

199	        else

201	        end
202	      end

205	      # Get current authenticated user's info
206	      #
207	      # Headers:
208	      #   Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
209	      #
210	      # Success response (200 OK):
211	      #   { "user": { "id": "...", "email": "...", ... } }
212	      def me
213	        render json: { user: user_payload(@current_user) }
214	      end

217	      # Change authenticated user's password
218	      #
219	      # Headers:

221	      #

235	      #
236	      # SECURITY:
237	      # - Requires valid JWT (authenticated endpoint)
238	      # - Verifies current password before allowing change
239	      # - Invalidates all refresh tokens (forces re-login on other devices)

242	        return render_password_mismatch_error unless password_confirmation_matches?
243	        return render_password_update_success if update_password
